pattern: Because every coordinate is squeezed into a tiny range, the whole universe of points that can ever be produced is bounded and small -- so the generations can simply be simulated set by set until the target shows up or the set stops growing at all.
hints: Keep the running set of every point produced so far (generations 0 through k-1); to build the next generation, take every pair of distinct points currently in that set and floor-average their coordinates.

Only count a point as newly found if it was not already present before this round -- once a round produces nothing that wasn't already there, the set can never grow again and the target is unreachable.
Because each coordinate is capped at a small maximum, the whole set of possible points is bounded, so this simulation always finishes quickly whether it succeeds or stalls.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, found in the first generation
statement example, found in the second generation
statement example, only one point so nothing can ever be generated
two points at the extreme corners of the coordinate range
the point set stalls without ever reaching the target
many duplicate-free points already covering the whole range
maximum number of initial points, all distinct

stress.description: random distinct starting points and a random target
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 20))
    pts = set()
    while len(pts) < n:
        pts.add((rng.randint(0, 6), rng.randint(0, 6), rng.randint(0, 6)))
    points = [list(p) for p in pts]
    target = [rng.randint(0, 6), rng.randint(0, 6), rng.randint(0, 6)]
    return [points, target]

complexity.time: O(B^2 * G) where B <= 343 is the bounded point universe and G the generations
